--- Thread (35 messages, 2018-06-26) ---
[2018-06-26 18:06] torikunsnaps (Torikun): how does one quickly and efficiently calc freeze?  need helping easing up the mental math
[2018-06-26 18:12] supergreyman (Greyman): If it's a small amount of freeze, I just add the attack threat to the HP amount that can be frozen. For example, I would say to myself, "Since he has 2 Shiver Yetis, that's enough to freeze my Wall, so I have to defend against X :Attack: +3.
[2018-06-26 18:12] supergreyman (Greyman): I usually calculate that way if he has a bunch of big freezers like spammed Nivo Charges.
[2018-06-26 18:14] supergreyman (Greyman): So I could say to myself, "He got 5 Nivo Charges, which is enough to freeze my 2 Walls, 1 Forcefield, and 2 Engineers. So that's 10HP frozen, so I need to defend against X :Attack: +10."
[2018-06-26 18:15] supergreyman (Greyman): If he's using a lot of granular freezers like Cryo Rays, simply add :Attack: with :Chill:
[2018-06-26 18:19] supergreyman (Greyman): You can also do mental math using subtraction instead of addition. That would be easier if your opponent is using a lot of big freeze where you are using big defenders as well. For example, if you're using a bunch of Xeno Guardians while he's using a lot of Tatsu, you can just add up :Attack: and :Chill: and then subtract 1 for each Xeno being chilled.
[2018-06-26 18:21] amalloy: this doesn't address the question of making sure you're still granular against the freeze. defending with E+Aegis+Xeno against 4 damage and 1 cryo is quite bad, for example
[2018-06-26 18:21] swapgo: I don't think there's a shortcut
[2018-06-26 18:23] supergreyman (Greyman): I think you just have to brute force possibilities in that case.
[2018-06-26 18:24] supergreyman (Greyman): Like, "What happen if he freezes Engineer."
[2018-06-26 18:25] supergreyman (Greyman): And by the time you figured it out, your time bank is already half gone. 😄
[2018-06-26 18:26] torikunsnaps (Torikun): I mean I normally visualize my opponent's turns but it's fairly slow and I time out pretty often, especially against stuff like endo
[2018-06-26 18:27] torikunsnaps (Torikun): I figure I should be calcing freeze differently
[2018-06-26 18:30] namington: just look at the variations of units they can feasibly chill
[2018-06-26 18:30] namington: for instance, if they have a frostbite and you only have rhinos
[2018-06-26 18:31] namington: they can only chill for 2 damage
[2018-06-26 18:31] namington: so you take 2 off your total defense and compare it to your opponent's attack
[2018-06-26 18:31] namington: similarly, if you have 2 engineers and a wall and they have a vai, you know they can chill a wall at max
[2018-06-26 18:31] namington: so you take 3 off your defense
[2018-06-26 18:31] namington: and compare to their attack (but 1 less, because vai consumes an attack to chill)
[2018-06-26 18:32] namington: if your opponent has an endotherm kit, usually chill is 1:1
[2018-06-26 18:32] supergreyman (Greyman): Still a lot of mental math nevertheless.
[2018-06-26 18:32] namington: that is to say, usually they'll figure out some combination of units that can chill your defences efficiently
[2018-06-26 18:32] namington: just cause, yknow, cryo ray
[2018-06-26 18:32] supergreyman (Greyman): I would recommend adding up attack and chill and then subtract.
[2018-06-26 18:33] namington: so if they have endotherm and you have 20 defense, you take off 16 defense cause they have 16 real chill
[2018-06-26 18:33] namington: assuming, of course, you have 4 units with health >= 3
[2018-06-26 18:34] namington: 8 forcefields, an engineer, and a wall

messages.author: torikunsnaps
messages.content: how does one quickly and efficiently calc freeze?  need helping easing up the mental math

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: If it's a small amount of freeze, I just add the attack threat to the HP amount that can be frozen. For example, I would say to myself, "Since he has 2 Shiver Yetis, that's enough to freeze my Wall, so I have to defend against X :Attack: +3.

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: I usually calculate that way if he has a bunch of big freezers like spammed Nivo Charges.

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: So I could say to myself, "He got 5 Nivo Charges, which is enough to freeze my 2 Walls, 1 Forcefield, and 2 Engineers. So that's 10HP frozen, so I need to defend against X :Attack: +10."

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: If he's using a lot of granular freezers like Cryo Rays, simply add :Attack: with :Chill:

messages.author: supergreyman
messages.content: You can also do mental math using subtraction instead of addition. That would be easier if your opponent is using a lot of big freeze where you are using big defenders as well. For example, if you're using a bunch of Xeno Guardians while he's using a lot of Tatsu, you can just add up :Attack: and :Chill: and then subtract 1 for each Xeno being chilled.
